Palmer Penguins Analysis

Patryk Basiak

Exploratory Data Analysis

View data

library(palmerpenguins)
## 
## Attaching package: 'palmerpenguins'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets':
## 
##     penguins, penguins_raw
data(penguins)
head(penguins)
## # A tibble: 6 × 8
##   species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
##   <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
## 1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
## 2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
## 3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250
## 4 Adelie  Torgersen           NA            NA                  NA          NA
## 5 Adelie  Torgersen           36.7          19.3               193        3450
## 6 Adelie  Torgersen           39.3          20.6               190        3650
## # ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>

Penguins species distrubtion

## 
## Attaching package: 'Hmisc'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     format.pval, units

Penguin sexes

Distrubtion here shows that sex cannot be a usefull paramter for prediction because in all species theres is almost 50-50 distrbution in sexes.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = sex, fill=species)) + geom_bar() + theme_minimal() + labs(title="Penguins sexes", 
     x = "Sex",
     y = "Amount")

Mass per species

Here we can clearly see that Gentoo are much more heavy than other species.

library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:Hmisc':
## 
##     src, summarize
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = body_mass_g, fill = species)) + geom_boxplot() + labs(title = "Distrubtion mass of penguins per species", y = "Mass", x = "Species") + theme_minimal()
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_boxplot()`).

colSums(is.na(penguins))
##           species            island    bill_length_mm     bill_depth_mm 
##                 0                 0                 2                 2 
## flipper_length_mm       body_mass_g               sex              year 
##                 2                 2                11                 0
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = island, fill = species)) + geom_bar() + labs(title = "Amount of penguins per island", y = "Amount", x = "Island") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = bill_length_mm, fill = species)) + geom_boxplot() + labs(title = "Bill length", y = "bill length (mm)", x = "Species") + theme_minimal()
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_boxplot()`).

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = bill_depth_mm, fill = species)) + geom_boxplot() + labs(title = "Bill depth", y = "bill depth (mm)", x = "Species") + theme_minimal()
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_boxplot()`).

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm, fill = species)) + geom_boxplot() + labs(title = "Flipper length", y = "flipper length (mm)", x = "Species") + theme_minimal()
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_boxplot()`).

Prediction

Data cleaning

penguins_smart <- na.omit(penguins)
ggplot(penguins_smart, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = flipper_length_mm, color = species, shape = island)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Relation beetwen lenght of bill to length of flipper",
       subtitle = "Visible speration beetwen species") +
  theme_minimal()

# Train test split

library(caret)
## Loading required package: lattice
set.seed(123)
train_index <- createDataPartition(penguins_smart$species, p = 0.8, list = FALSE)

train_data <- penguins_smart[train_index, ]
test_data  <- penguins_smart[-train_index, ]

Random Forest

library(randomForest)
## randomForest 4.7-1.2
## Type rfNews() to see new features/changes/bug fixes.
## 
## Attaching package: 'randomForest'
## The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     combine
## The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2':
## 
##     margin
rf_model <- randomForest(species ~ bill_length_mm + bill_depth_mm + flipper_length_mm + body_mass_g + island, data = train_data,
                         importance = TRUE)

print(rf_model)
## 
## Call:
##  randomForest(formula = species ~ bill_length_mm + bill_depth_mm +      flipper_length_mm + body_mass_g + island, data = train_data,      importance = TRUE) 
##                Type of random forest: classification
##                      Number of trees: 500
## No. of variables tried at each split: 2
## 
##         OOB estimate of  error rate: 1.49%
## Confusion matrix:
##           Adelie Chinstrap Gentoo class.error
## Adelie       115         2      0  0.01709402
## Chinstrap      2        53      0  0.03636364
## Gentoo         0         0     96  0.00000000
predictions <- predict(rf_model, test_data)

accuracy <- mean(predictions == test_data$species)

print(accuracy)
## [1] 0.9846154
table(Predicted = predictions, Actual = test_data$species)
##            Actual
## Predicted   Adelie Chinstrap Gentoo
##   Adelie        29         1      0
##   Chinstrap      0        12      0
##   Gentoo         0         0     23
varImpPlot(rf_model)

# Conclusion

The Palmer Penguins dataset consists of various morphological parameters, including bill length, bill depth, flipper length, and body mass. The Random Forest model proved to be highly effective, achieving an accuracy of approximately 98.46% in classifying the three species.

Analysis of variable importance indicates that bill_length_mm is the most critical predictor for species differentiation. Futhermore, body_mass_g was found to be the least influential variable. This result is consistent with the exploratory analysis, which shows a significant overlap in body mass between the Adelie and Chinstrap species, making it a less reliable metric for classification compared to bill dimensions.
